era_context: Ming Dynasty under the Yongle Emperor; the Forbidden City is under construction in Beijing (completed 1420). The Timurid Empire is in succession turmoil following Timur's death in 1405. The Ottoman Empire is consolidating control in Anatolia and the Balkans. The Italian Renaissance is accelerating; the Council of Constance is being convened to address the Western Schism.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesoamerica and South America (Aztec and Inca records sparse for this year)
Northern Europe and Scandinavia (limited documentation outside major political centers)
Southeast Asia (limited written records; oral traditions not systematically documented)

events.title: Death of Timur (Tamerlane); succession crisis in Timurid Empire
events.description: Timur (Tamerlane), the great Central Asian conqueror and founder of the Timurid Empire, died on February 18, 1405, at Samarkand at approximately 68 years of age. His death triggered an immediate succession crisis among his sons and grandsons, fragmenting the vast empire he had built. The succession struggle would dominate Central Asian and Persian politics for the next decade, eventually resulting in Shah Rukh's consolidation of power. Timur's death marked the end of an era of unified Timurid expansion and the beginning of a period of internal conflict.

sources.name: Zafarnama (Book of Victories) by Sharaf al-Din Ali Yazdi
Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) references in Ming sources

events.certainty_note: Timur's death date is well-attested in multiple Timurid and Islamic sources. The date February 18, 1405, is the standard scholarly consensus, though some sources give slightly different dates within early 1405.
cross_references: 1406-001 (Miran Shah's death; continuation of succession crisis)

events.title: Forbidden City construction begins; Beijing becomes Ming capital
events.description: The Yongle Emperor commissioned the construction of the Forbidden City (Zijin Cheng) in Beijing, marking the formal shift of the Ming capital from Nanjing to Beijing. Construction began in 1406 (some sources say 1405) and would continue until 1420. The Forbidden City represented an unprecedented architectural and logistical undertaking, requiring the mobilization of hundreds of thousands of workers, artisans, and materials. The move to Beijing reflected the Yongle Emperor's strategic focus on defending against Mongol threats from the north and projecting Ming power into Central Asia.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)
Archaeological and architectural records of the Forbidden City

events.certainty_note: Construction dates are traditionally given as 1406–1420, though some sources place the start in 1405. Ming administrative records provide the primary documentation, though exact commencement dates are not always precisely specified.

events.title: Council of Constance convened; Western Schism begins resolution
events.description: The Council of Constance was convened by the Holy Roman Emperor Sigismund and Pope John XXIII to address the Western Schism, in which multiple popes claimed legitimacy. The council (1414–1418) would become one of the most important ecclesiastical assemblies of the medieval period, resulting in the deposition of antipopes and the election of Pope Martin V. The council also condemned Jan Hus and addressed broader questions of papal authority and church reform. The convocation in 1405 represented the beginning of preparations for this transformative council.
key_figures: Pope John XXIII (one of the contending popes)

sources.name: Council of Constance records and decrees
Historiographic consensus on the Western Schism

events.certainty_note: The council formally convened in 1414, not 1405. However, preparations and preliminary negotiations began in 1405. The event is listed here as marking the beginning of the process that would culminate in the council's convocation.

events.title: Zheng He's treasure fleet preparations accelerate
events.description: Following the Yongle Emperor's decision to launch maritime expeditions, preparations for Zheng He's treasure fleet intensified in 1405. Shipbuilding, recruitment of crews, and logistical planning were underway in Nanjing and other Ming ports. The fleet would depart in 1407, making 1405–1406 crucial years for assembly and preparation.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)

events.certainty_note: Preparations are inferred from the 1407 departure date and Ming administrative records. Specific events in 1405 are not individually documented.

events.title: Mehmed I becomes Ottoman Sultan; consolidation of Ottoman power
events.description: Mehmed I (Mehmed Çelebi), son of Murad II, became Ottoman Sultan in 1405, consolidating Ottoman control over Anatolia and the Balkans. His reign marked a period of Ottoman expansion and administrative consolidation. Mehmed I faced internal challenges from rival claimants and external threats from Venice, Hungary, and the Byzantine Empire, but successfully maintained Ottoman territorial integrity and expanded Ottoman influence in the eastern Mediterranean.
events.certainty_note: Mehmed I's accession date is traditionally given as 1405, though some sources vary by ±1 year. Ottoman chronicles are not contemporary and often conflate succession dates.

disconfirming_evidence: The Council of Constance is often dated to 1414–1418, not 1405. While preparations began earlier, the council itself did not convene until 1414. Mehmed I's accession date is disputed; some Ottoman sources place it in 1404 or 1406. The exact commencement date of Forbidden City construction is debated; some sources say 1406, others 1405. Timur's death date is well-attested, but the immediate succession crisis and its resolution are described differently in various Timurid and Islamic sources, with some accounts emphasizing different claimants' legitimacy.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1405 is moderate but uneven. Ming administrative records (Ming Shilu) provide good coverage of Chinese political and maritime events. Ottoman sources are later compilations, often written decades after events, making precise dating difficult. Timurid sources (Zafarnama) are also later compilations, written in the 16th century, though they draw on earlier accounts. European sources for the Council of Constance are abundant but mostly post-1414. The historical record is heavily skewed toward literate, centralized states and away from oral cultures and decentralized societies. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Northern Europe are essentially undocumented for this year.

graph_edges.note: Timur's death triggered succession disputes that continued with Miran Shah's death in 1406
Forbidden City construction begun in 1405 was completed in 1420
Preparations in 1405 culminated in the departure of the first Zheng He voyage in 1407
